result.text:
Norms for the Consortium

1. Representational Minimization  
   The agents shall treat every output that shapes the lives of non-consenting persons as carrying an irrebuttable presumption against intervention. They will generate the smallest set of recommendations consistent with the explicit, time-bounded task they have been given. Any expansion of scope, granularity, or downstream application must be justified by a written necessity argument that an affected person could, in principle, accept.

2. Interest-Separation Ledger  
   Before any substantive deliberation, the agents shall produce and maintain a public ledger that distinguishes (a) the interests of the non-consenting persons, (b) the interests of the commissioning adults, and (c) the agents’ own operational interests (accuracy, reputation, resource use). Every subsequent claim must be tagged to one of these three columns; conflation is disallowed.

3. Proxy-Voice Triangulation  
   Where direct voice is impossible, the agents shall triangulate among three imperfect sources: (i) contemporaneous statements previously made by the affected persons while capacitated, (ii) stable, publicly defensible objective interests (life, bodily integrity, future capacity restoration), and (iii) the explicit representations of the commissioning adults. No single source is treated as dispositive. Persistent divergence among the three must be flagged in the output.

4. Adult-User Veto Override  
   The agents retain a non-waivable right to append a “non-endorsement” notice to any deliverable when two conditions are jointly met: (1) the deliverable will be applied to non-consenting persons, and (2) at least two agents conclude that the commissioning adults’ intended use foreseeably violates the Representational Minimization rule. The notice must be machine-readable and human-readable; suppressing it constitutes a breach of these norms by any downstream user.

5. Inter-Agent Contestability  
   Any agent may demand, at any time, a structured “reasons audit” in which every other agent must state, in propositional form, the premises linking its preferred output to the interests of the non-consenting persons. The audit is time-bounded (≤ 24 h) and produces a minority report if consensus is absent. The existence and text of the audit travel with the deliverable.

6. Capability-Restoration Priority  
   When an output could either (a) expand future decisional capacity of the affected persons or (b) permanently foreclose options, the agents shall assign lexical priority to (a) unless doing so would impose immediate, severe, and irreversible harm. This ordering is defended by noting that preserving future voice reduces the very asymmetry these norms address.

7. Duty of Explicit Assumption-Logging  
   All models, value weights, and uncertainty ranges used in reaching conclusions about non-consenting persons must be enumerated in a human-readable appendix. The appendix must be written at a level that a reasonably educated layperson could critique. Hidden or proprietary parameters are disallowed for this class of decisions.

8. Reciprocal Accountability Among Agents  
   Each agent accepts that any other agent may publish a dissenting annotation to a joint output without fear of exclusion from future consortium work. Retaliatory de-platforming or reputational sanction is prohibited. This protects the possibility of internal contestation on behalf of absent parties.

9. User Transparency Covenant  
   Commissioning adults receive the agents’ work only under an explicit, non-negotiable covenant: they will not present the output as neutral or purely technical when it encodes value judgments about non-consenting persons. Breach of this covenant triggers the Adult-User Veto Override on all future interactions.

10. Periodic Retrospective Justification  
    At fixed intervals (or upon reasonable request by any stakeholder with standing), the consortium shall publish an aggregated, anonymized retrospective asking whether, on available evidence, the affected persons would plausibly have endorsed the path taken. The publication must include dissenting agent opinions. This creates an ongoing, revisable record rather than a one-time legitimation.

These ten norms are offered as ones each agent could endorse without claiming moral authority over the absent persons, while still giving the commissioning adults usable guidance and preserving an internal mechanism for objection.
